## TOP NEWS (recent, top 6)
Each item: title (source, date) + URL + Excerpt. Cite the URL when handing it to the user.
- "White Sox rookie Murakami hits MLB-best 13th HR" (ESPN MLB, 2026-05-02)
  https://www.espn.com/mlb/story/_/id/48652862/munetaka-murakami-hits-13th-homer-season-win-padres
  Excerpt: Munetaka Murakami hit his majors-leading 13th homer of the season to highlight a six-run second inning, and the White Sox beat the Padres 8-2 on Friday night.

## CARDINALS DEEP (curated retired numbers, HOFers, historic seasons, traditions)
Retired numbers: #1 Ozzie Smith (1996), #2 Red Schoendienst (1996), #6 Stan Musial (1963), #9 Enos Slaughter (1996), #14 Ken Boyer (1984), #17 Dizzy Dean (1974), #20 Lou Brock (1979), #24 Whitey Herzog (2010), #42 Bruce Sutter (2006), #45 Bob Gibson (1975), #85 August Busch Jr. (1984).
Hall of Fame Cardinals (19 total):
- Rogers Hornsby (1915-1926) · HOF 1942: Career .358 BA, second only to Cobb. Two Triple Crowns.
- Grover Cleveland Alexander (1926-1929) · HOF 1938: Game 7 strikeout of Tony Lazzeri sealed 1926 WS.
- Jim Bottomley (1922-1932) · HOF 1974: 1928 NL MVP.
- Frankie Frisch (1927-1937) · HOF 1947: Player-manager of the 1934 Gas House Gang.
- Chick Hafey (1924-1931) · HOF 1971: 1931 NL batting champion.
- Dizzy Dean (1930-1937) · HOF 1953: 30-7 in 1934, last NL 30-game winner.
- Joe Medwick (1932-1940) · HOF 1968: 1937 NL Triple Crown winner.
- Johnny Mize (1936-1941) · HOF 1981: Three-time NL HR leader.
- Enos Slaughter (1938-1953) · HOF 1985: 'Mad Dash' to score winning run in Game 7 of 1946 WS.
- Stan Musial (1941-1963) · HOF 1969: The standard Cardinal. 24-time All-Star.
Historic seasons:
- 1926 — First championship: Rogers Hornsby player-manager. Grover Cleveland Alexander — picked up mid-season — struck out Tony Lazzeri in Game 7 with the bases loaded to preserve the title.
- 1934 — The Gas House Gang: Dizzy Dean 30-7, brother Paul 19-11. Beat Detroit in 7. Joe Medwick ejected from Game 7 for his own safety after being pelted with fruit by Tigers fans.
- 1942 — Musial era begins: Stan Musial's first full season. Cardinals won 106 games and beat the Yankees in 5 — New York's first WS loss since 1926.
- 1944 — Streetcar Series: Cardinals beat the St. Louis Browns in the only all-St. Louis World Series, played entirely at Sportsman's Park.
- 1946 — The Mad Dash: Enos Slaughter scored from first on a single in the 8th of Game 7 against the Red Sox, winning the title. Musial's first postseason MVP-caliber season.
- 1964 — Gibson arrives: Philadelphia's late-season collapse handed the Cardinals the pennant. Bob Gibson won Games 5 and 7 over the Yankees. Ken Boyer won NL MVP.
Traditions:
- The Rally Squirrel: During Game 4 of the 2011 NLDS at Busch Stadium, a squirrel ran across home plate during a pitch. It returned for Game 5 and became the unofficial mascot of the 2011 championship r
- Clydesdales: The Anheuser-Busch Clydesdales circle the warning track on Opening Day and for special occasions — a tradition dating to 1933 when August Busch Sr. used Clydesdales to deliver the 
- Two birds on the bat: The classic Cardinals logo — two cardinals perched on a baseball bat — was inspired by Allie May Schmidt, who saw cardinals at a church picnic in 1921 and sketched the image. Used 
## CURATED STORIES (selected baseball narratives)
- Bob Feller: First MLB Player to Enlist After Pearl Harbor (WWII): Cleveland's 23-year-old ace volunteered on December 8, 1941 and served four years as a Navy gunnery officer aboard the USS Alabama.
- Ted Williams: Marine Pilot in Two Wars (WWII/Korea): The last .400 hitter missed nearly five prime seasons flying combat missions — including 39 in Korea, several as John Glenn's wingman.
- Warren Spahn: Bronze Star at the Battle of the Bulge (WWII): The winningest left-hander in MLB history earned a battlefield commission and the Bronze Star defending a critical bridge.
- Yogi Berra: Gunner's Mate at D-Day (WWII): Nineteen years old and a career minor-leaguer, the future Yankees catcher fired rockets off Omaha Beach on June 6, 1944.
- Jerry Coleman: The Only MLB Player to See Combat in Two Wars (WWII/Korea): A Yankees All-Star and later a Padres broadcaster, Coleman flew 120 combat missions across WWII and Korea.
- Jackie Robinson: Court-Martialed for Refusing a Bus Driver (WWII): Two years before Rosa Parks, a young Army lieutenant refused to move to the back of a military bus — and was acquitted at court-martial.
- Roberto Clemente: Killed Delivering Aid to Nicaragua (1970s): On New Year's Eve 1972, Clemente boarded an overloaded plane carrying earthquake relief to Managua. It never arrived.
- Hank Greenberg: First MLB Player Drafted for WWII (WWII): The reigning AL MVP was drafted in 1941, discharged on December 5, then re-enlisted two days after Pearl Harbor.
## MLB LEGENDS (broader hall-of-fame coverage)
- Vin Scully (Broadcaster, 1950-2016): Voice of the Dodgers for 67 seasons — the longest tenure with one team in sports broadcasting history. Called Hank Aaron's 715th HR, Koufax's perfect game, Gibson's 1988 walk-off, and countless other 
- Sparky Anderson (Manager, 1970-1995): First manager to win a World Series in both leagues (Reds 1975-76, Tigers 1984). 2,194 career wins. Architect of the Big Red Machine. Hall of Fame 2000.
- Connie Mack (Manager / Owner, 1894-1950): Managed the Philadelphia Athletics for 50 consecutive seasons (1901-1950). 3,731 career wins — the all-time record, likely never to be broken. Five World Series championships. Hall of Fame 1937.
- Casey Stengel (Manager, 1934-1965): Managed seven Yankees World Series champions (1949-1953 consecutive + 1956, 1958). Also led the expansion 1962 Mets. Famously quotable 'Stengelese.' Hall of Fame 1966.
- Kenesaw Mountain Landis (Commissioner, 1920-1944): First MLB commissioner, appointed after the 1919 Black Sox scandal. Banned the eight implicated White Sox players for life. Reshaped baseball's governance. Hall of Fame 1944.
- Bill Veeck (Owner / Showman, 1941-1980): Innovative owner of the Indians, Browns, and White Sox. Signed Larry Doby in 1947 to integrate the American League. Sent 3'7" Eddie Gaedel to the plate in 1951. Invented the exploding scoreboard. Hall
- Oscar Charleston (Outfielder / Manager, 1915-1941): Widely considered the greatest all-around Negro Leagues player. Bill James ranked him among the 10 best players in baseball history. Powerful left-handed hitter with speed and defensive brilliance. Ha
- Josh Gibson (Catcher, 1930-1946): Widely considered the greatest catcher of all time. Negro Leagues career batting average reported above .370. MLB officially recognized his Negro Leagues statistics in 2020 — after that, he holds mult
- Buck O'Neil (First Baseman / Manager / Ambassador, 1937-1962 (playing) · 1962-2006 (beyond)): Kansas City Monarchs first baseman and later the first Black coach in MLB history (Cubs, 1962). Tireless Negro Leagues historian and ambassador for 40+ years after his playing career. Posthumous Hall 
- Cool Papa Bell (Outfielder, 1922-1946): Famed for his speed. Satchel Paige said he was 'so fast he could turn off the light and be in bed before the room got dark.' Hall of Fame 1974.
## STRANGEST PLAYS IN BASEBALL HISTORY
- Eddie Gaedel: the 3'7" pinch hitter (1951-08-19): Browns owner Bill Veeck sent 3'7" Eddie Gaedel to pinch-hit wearing uniform #1/8. He walked on four pitches and was pulled for a pinch runner. MLB voided his contract the next day. Career line: 1 plat
- Disco Demolition Night (1979-07-12): Between games of a doubleheader, a radio DJ blew up a crate of disco records in center field. Tens of thousands of fans stormed the field. The White Sox forfeited game two to Detroit — one of only a h
- Ten Cent Beer Night (1974-06-04): Cleveland offered 10-cent beer as a promotion. Thousands of intoxicated fans stormed the field in the 9th. Umpire Nestor Chylak declared a forfeit to the Rangers. MLB banned discount-alcohol promotion
- Rick Monday saves the flag (1976-04-25): Two protesters ran onto Dodger Stadium's outfield and attempted to set fire to an American flag. Cubs center fielder Rick Monday sprinted over and snatched it away before they could light it. Honored 
- Dock Ellis's LSD no-hitter (1970-06-12): Dock Ellis later said he was under the influence of LSD — believing he was pitching at the wrong stadium — when he threw a no-hitter against the Padres. He walked 8 batters and hit 1, but allowed no h
- Fred Merkle's Boner (1908-09-23): With two out in the 9th, Giants runner Fred Merkle failed to touch second base on what appeared to be the winning hit. The Cubs appealed; umpires called him out, negating the run. The tied game was re
- José Canseco's head home run (1993-05-26): A fly ball to right field bounced off José Canseco's head and over the fence for a home run. Canseco joked about it for the rest of his career. The play is still one of the most replayed outfield bloo
- Randy Johnson vs. the bird (2001-03-24): During a spring-training game, Randy Johnson delivered a fastball that struck a dove mid-flight, killing the bird instantly. Both teams watched in stunned silence. The at-bat was recorded as no pitch.
## BROTHERS IN MLB
- Joe, Dom, and Vince DiMaggio (1937-1953): Three brothers in MLB outfields in the 1940s.
- Felipe, Matty, and Jesús Alou (1958-1979): On September 10 and 15, 1963, all three Alou brothers played in the same Giants outfield simultaneously — the only time three brothers have done so in MLB history.
- Paul and Lloyd Waner (1926-1945): Both Hall of Famers. Both Pittsburgh Pirates. Roughly 5,600 combined career hits — among the most by any brother pair in MLB history.
- Dizzy and Paul "Daffy" Dean (1930-1941): 49 combined wins for the 1934 Gas House Gang Cardinals (Dizzy 30-7, Paul 19-11). Each brother won two games in the 1934 World Series.
- The five Delahanty brothers (1888-1915): Ed, Frank, Jim, Joe, and Tom Delahanty — the only five-brother combination to appear in MLB. Ed's career .346 batting average is still among the highest ever recorded.
- Phil and Joe Niekro (1964-1987): 539 combined career wins — the most by any brother pair in MLB history. Both knuckleball specialists.
- Gaylord and Jim Perry (1959-1983): The only brothers to both win a Cy Young Award. Gaylord remains one of very few pitchers to win the award in both leagues.
- Bengie, José, and Yadier Molina (1998-2022): Three catching brothers, all World Series champions.
## TRIVIA (sample, 30 questions in dataset)
- Q: Who holds the MLB single-season home run record?
  A: Barry Bonds — 73 in 2001 (San Francisco Giants).
- Q: How many World Series titles have the St. Louis Cardinals won?
  A: 11 (1926, 1931, 1934, 1942, 1944, 1946, 1964, 1967, 1982, 2006, 2011). Second most in MLB, behind the Yankees.
- Q: Who was the last player to hit .400 in a season?
  A: Ted Williams — .406 in 1941 (Boston Red Sox). No one has done it since.
- Q: What team did Jackie Robinson debut with when he broke the MLB color barrier?
  A: Brooklyn Dodgers — April 15, 1947, at Ebbets Field against the Boston Braves.
- Q: Who holds the record for most career stolen bases?
  A: Rickey Henderson — 1,406 career steals. Lou Brock is second with 938.
- Q: Which player's consecutive-games-played streak stood at 2,130 from 1939 to 1995?
  A: Lou Gehrig. Cal Ripken Jr. passed it on September 6, 1995, finishing at 2,632.
## MLB FRANCHISES (30 teams)

NL: St. Louis Cardinals (STL), Washington Nationals (WSN), Atlanta Braves (ATL), Miami Marlins (MIA), New York Mets (NYM), Philadelphia Phillies (PHI), Chicago Cubs (CHC), Cincinnati Reds (CIN), Milwaukee Brewers (MIL), Pittsburgh Pirates (PIT), Arizona Diamondbacks (ARI), Colorado Rockies (COL), Los Angeles Dodgers (LAD), San Diego Padres (SD), San Francisco Giants (SF)
## PLAYER DIRECTORY
The site indexes 23614 players from Chadwick (1871-2026). Source: Chadwick Bureau Register (public domain).
For a specific player, the user can search the Players tab. Do not invent player details — if a player isn't mentioned elsewhere in this context bundle, say "I don't have details on that player in today's context — try the Players tab on the site for the searchable directory."
---
END OF CONTEXT BUNDLE. If a question cannot be answered from the above, say so honestly. Do not fabricate stats, dates, or biographical facts.
